hero_summary: Maqam Hijazayn is less widely circulated today, but its theoretical material agrees on one essential point: it is built on two connected Hijaz ajnas, and that is precisely why it is called Hijazayn, or “two Hijazes.”
short_identity: A branch of the Hijaz family built on Hijaz on Dukah (D), then Hijaz on Nawa (G).

quick_facts.common_start: It usually begins by stabilizing Hijaz on Dukah (D), then rises to the fifth to confirm the second Hijaz
quick_facts.character: A doubled Hijaz character; the Hijaz color remains dominant in both the original and the upper branch

sayr.summary: The practical sayr of Hijazayn usually works as follows: it begins from Jins Hijaz on Dukah (D) and clearly stabilizes the tonic, then rises to Nawa (G). But instead of changing color there to Nahawand or Rast, it settles a second Hijaz on Nawa. That is where the true difference of the maqam appears. From there the line may extend to Kardan (upper C) and beyond, before returning at the end to Dukah (D).
resting_tones: Dukah (D): the final tonic, because the original jins is Hijaz on Dukah
Nawa (G): the most important secondary point of rest, because the second Hijaz begins there and marks the maqam
Kardan (upper C): a natural upper jawab summit when the maqam expands higher
motion_notes: Hijazayn is not ordinary Hijaz; it is a maqam that keeps the Hijaz color dominant in both the original and upper branch
In the more familiar Hijaz, the upper region tends toward Nahawand on Nawa, but here it opens a second Hijaz instead
One should not open Nahawand on Nawa if the intended maqam is Hijazayn
Al-Khula‘i’s old expression ‘from two Hijazes in two diwans’ aligns perfectly with this structural understanding
common_path: Settle the Hijaz on the fifth clearly
Expand to Kardan (upper C) when needed

common_modulations: Hijaz on Nawa (G) as the second Hijaz that distinguishes the maqam
If Nahawand opens on the fifth, the ear returns toward the more familiar Hijaz
The value of the maqam here lies in keeping the Hijaz color present in both diwans
The available documentation here is stronger in structural definition than in listing famous agreed-upon Arabic repertoire examples

reference_recordings.title: At this stage there are no strongly documented Arabic repertoire examples
reference_recordings.note: What is available here is a clear structural definition, together with an old historical attribution to the idea of two Hijazes in two diwans, but not the same level of practical repertoire evidence available for some other maqamat
